PUBLIC ACTS
OP
THE STATE OF KENTUCKY ,
PASSED AT THE ADJOURNED SESSION OF THE
GENERAL ASSEMBLY (JANUARY 3, 1867) WHICH
WAS BEGUN AND HELD IN THE CITY OF
FRANKFORT , ON MONDAY , THE 4TH

classification.title: AN ACT to increase the jurisdiction of the Jefferson Court of Common Pleas.
classification.summary: This act repeals certain sections of an act establishing the levy and county court for Jefferson County, transferring their jurisdiction in ordinary and equity actions to the Jefferson Court of Common Pleas. It also mandates the transfer of pending suits and bonds from the county court to the court of common pleas, with exceptions for bastardy cases. The act clarifies that appeals and traverses from justices of the peace within Jefferson County will now go to the Jefferson Court of Common Pleas. The jurisdiction of the county court as a levy or probate court remains unchanged.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the transfer of judicial jurisdiction between courts in Jefferson County, Kentucky. It details procedural changes related to civil actions, appeals, and the handling of legal bonds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The exception for 'bastardy cases' is a legal category and not inherently racial. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to abolish the July term of the Clark Circuit Court.
classification.summary: This act abolishes the July term of the Clark Circuit Court, which was held annually for the trial of criminal and equity causes. All legal processes, subpoenas, bail bonds, and recognizances that were returnable to the July term are now to be made returnable to the November term. Persons summoned or obligated to appear at the July term are required to appear at the November term instead.
classification.reasoning: The statute's title and text clearly indicate that it concerns the procedural scheduling of the Clark Circuit Court. It deals with the abolition of a specific court term and the rescheduling of legal obligations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly create racial distinctions or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to establish Courts of Common Pleas in certain Judicial Districts
classification.summary: This act establishes Courts of Common Pleas in specific judicial districts of Kentucky, outlining their jurisdiction, the duties of sheriffs and clerks, and the transfer of cases from circuit courts. The courts are granted original jurisdiction in civil matters, similar to circuit courts, but explicitly excluded from criminal or penal jurisdiction. They also have appellate jurisdiction in civil actions and traverses of writs of forcible entry and detainer. The act details the responsibilities of sheriffs and clerks in these new courts and specifies procedures for transferring pending civil cases from circuit courts to the courts of common pleas.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the establishment and jurisdiction of a new court system (Courts of Common Pleas) within Kentucky. It details the procedural aspects of civil litigation, including jurisdiction, the roles of court officers, and the transfer of cases. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it appears to be an act passed by the General Assembly in 1867.

classification.summary: This act establishes and defines the powers and procedures for Courts of Common Pleas within judicial districts. It outlines the roles of jailers and coroners, the election and qualification of pro tem judges, and grants judges the power to issue writs such as habeas corpus, mandamus, and prohibition. The statute also details procedures for venue changes and the consolidation of cases pending in circuit courts and courts of common pleas, as well as jury selection and the application of the Code of Practice.

The language and subject matter are purely procedural and administrative, relating to the functioning of the court system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Liability of decedents' estates and organization of companies
classification.summary: This statute addresses two distinct matters: first, it clarifies that the estates and funds held by executors, administrators, guardians, or trustees are liable only to the extent the deceased or incapacitated person would have been if alive. Second, it outlines the process for companies operating under special charters to dissolve and reorganize under the current act, including provisions for assuming names, transferring assets and liabilities, and filing necessary certificates with state and county officials. The existence of such a corporation is limited to fifty years.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided discusses the liability of estates and the process for companies to reorganize. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from '1867'.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend Chapter 34 of the Revised Statutes,title "Escheats and Escheators."
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 34 of the Revised Statutes concerning Escheats and Escheators. It specifies that if a free person of color dies intestate, and their heirs would have inherited but were previously held in slavery, or if a free person of color dies leaving a will that bequeaths property to individuals who were previously held in slavery, these heirs, legatees, and devisees (or their heirs) are now competent to inherit or receive the property.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'free person of color' and refers to individuals 'held in slavery.' While this act appears to be rectifying a past injustice by allowing formerly enslaved individuals to inherit, it is still a statute that directly addresses racial categories and the legacy of slavery. Therefore, it falls under the scope of Jim Crow era legislation as it deals with the legal status and rights of Black individuals in the post-slavery era, even if in a seemingly remedial way. The confidence is high because of the explicit mention of race and the historical context of the period. The category is 'other' as it pertains to property inheritance and not a typical Jim Crow category like segregation or voting rights.

classification.title: AN ACT to relieve the tax-payers of the State from the payment of ten per cent. extra commission to the Sheriffs or Collectors of Revenue.
classification.summary: This act repeals a provision that allowed sheriffs or collectors to charge an additional ten percent commission on taxes not paid by a certain deadline. The original provision, enacted in 1862, stipulated that if taxes were not paid by October 15th, they would incur a ten percent penalty if not paid by December 15th, with this penalty being retained by the sheriff/collector as part of their commission.
classification.reasoning: The statute is from the Laws of Kentucky, 1867, and is titled 'AN ACT to relieve the tax-payers of the State from the payment of ten per cent. extra commission to the Sheriffs or Collectors of Revenue.' It specifically repeals a section of a previous act that imposed a ten percent extra commission on late tax payments, which was to be retained by the sheriff or collector. The text of the statute and its referenced section do not contain any mention of race, nor do they imply any racial discrimination in their application. The focus is solely on tax collection procedures and commissions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An act to ascertain and settle the accounts of certain officers and soldiers
classification.summary: This act provides a mechanism for settling the accounts of officers and soldiers who served the State of Kentucky under specific acts during the Civil War, including those who died in service before being mustered into U.S. service, or were discharged due to physical disability. It outlines payment procedures to the individuals, their widows, or children, and specifies that the funds are not subject to attachment or assignment. It also clarifies that the act embraces certain quartermasters, commissaries, surgeons, and soldiers with disabilities, but not recruiting officers who failed to enlist.

It outlines procedures for payment to soldiers, their widows, or children, and addresses cases of disability or death in service. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. For Lincoln County, terms will begin on the third Monday of April and October, lasting twelve juridical days. For Casey County, terms will begin on the second Monday of March and September, lasting six juridical days. The act specifies that it will not interfere with the spring terms of these courts for the year 1867 as already established.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to provide for auditing and paying Military Claims."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning military claims. It expands the duties of the Quarter-Master General to audit unpaid claims against the State arising from military service of State troops from 1862 onwards, including claims for rent, transportation, services rendered, quartermaster's stores, and arrears of pay. Specific conditions are outlined for auditing rent and transportation claims, requiring authorization by a commissioned officer. It also clarifies the types of quartermaster's stores that can be claimed. The act further mandates reporting audited claims to the General Assembly and construes existing laws to allow payment for subsistence furnished to recruits for Kentucky regiments, provided they were under the charge of an authorized recruiting officer.

classification.title: AN ACT requiring Judges of the County Courts and Justices of the Peace to execute Bond
classification.summary: This act requires judges of county courts, justices of the peace, and police judges in Kentucky to execute a bond or obligation within sixty days of the act's passage. This bond, payable to the Commonwealth and secured by sufficient surety approved by the clerk of the circuit court (for county judges) or county court (for justices and police judges), ensures the faithful discharge of their duties and the proper handling of any money received in their official capacity. These bonds are to be recorded and renewed every two years or as required by the court.
classification.reasoning: The statute requires judicial and quasi-judicial officers to execute bonds to ensure faithful performance of their duties and proper handling of funds. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1867.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the support of paupers and the education of children of this Commonwealth."
classification.summary: This act levies an additional tax of two dollars on every male negro and mulatto over eighteen years of age. The collected funds are to be used exclusively for the support of negro and mulatto paupers and the education of negro and mulatto children within the county where the tax was collected. It mandates separate tax columns for negroes and mulattoes and outlines the process for allocating funds to negro schools, including per-scholar allowances and reporting requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'negro and mulatto' in relation to taxation and education. It establishes a separate fund derived from a tax specifically levied on 'male negro and mulatto over the age of eighteen years.' The funds are designated for the support of 'negro and mulatto paupers' and the 'education of negro and mulatto children.' This clear segregation of funds and services based on race, even if for the stated purpose of providing separate support, is characteristic of Jim Crow era legislation aimed at maintaining racial separation and control. classification.title: RESOLUTIONS in regard to electionof United StatesSenator and proposed Amendment of the Constitution of the United States
classification.summary: This document contains two resolutions passed by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky in 1867. Resolution No. 36 rescinds a previous order for the election of a United States Senator and sets a new date for the election, to be conducted in conformity with an act of Congress. Resolution No. 37 addresses the proposed Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, quoting its text regarding citizenship, privileges and immunities, due process, and equal protection of the laws, as well as provisions for apportionment of representatives and voting rights.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two resolutions. The first resolution deals with the procedural aspect of electing a U.S. Senator. The second resolution quotes the text of the proposed Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. While the Fourteenth Amendment is foundational to civil rights and was enacted in the post-Civil War era, the resolution itself is merely acknowledging and quoting the amendment. It does not enact any discriminatory laws or policies. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is a resolution passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates it's a procedural resolution and a quote of a constitutional amendment, not an enactment of discriminatory legislation.

classification.title: Resolution Rejecting the 14th Amendment
classification.summary: This resolution from the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky officially rejects the proposed amendment to the United States Constitution (likely the 14th Amendment, given the context of 1867 and the content of Section 4 regarding debts and emancipation of slaves). The legislature requests the Governor to notify the appropriate federal departments of this rejection.
classification.reasoning: The statute is a resolution by the Kentucky General Assembly rejecting a proposed amendment to the U.S. Constitution. While the text of the proposed amendment (quoted within the resolution) mentions 'debts incurred for payment of pensions and bounties for services in suppressing insurrection or rebellion' and explicitly states that 'neither the United States nor any State shall assume or pay any debt or obligation incurred in aid of insurrection or rebellion against the United States, or any claim for the loss or emancipation of any slave; but all such debts, obligations, and claims shall be held illegal and void,' this statute itself is not a Jim Crow law. It is a legislative action regarding a federal amendment. The mention of 'emancipation of any slave' indicates a racial context related to the post-Civil War era, but the resolution's purpose is to reject the amendment, not to enact discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'amendments' because it concerns a proposed constitutional amendment. The racial indicator is 'implicit' due to the reference to slave emancipation, which is tied to race, but the statute itself does not enact racial discrimination.

classification.summary: This resolution mandates that the Clerk of each House of the Kentucky General Assembly must indorse on any bill appropriating over one hundred dollars that it was passed by a call of yeas and nays and by a constitutional majority. This indorsement is to be copied onto the enrolled bill.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute is a resolution concerning legislative procedure for bills appropriating money. It requires a record of how such bills were passed (yeas and nays, constitutional majority) to be noted on the bill. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The category is 'other' as it doesn't fit neatly into the provided categories and is not a direct Jim Crow law concerning segregation or disenfranchisement, but rather a racialized administrative provision.

The marshal must execute a bond with security, approved by the trustees. The trustees are empowered to fill any vacancies in the marshal's office.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the Hope Insurance Company, of Louisville.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Hope Insurance Company of Louisville. It modifies section twenty-four of the original act, concerning the company's capital stock. The amendment clarifies that cash premiums constitute the initial capital stock, which can be increased by a guarantee capital. It also authorizes the board of directors to establish a stock department, open subscription books for stock up to $500,000, divided into $100 shares, and set terms for cash payments and notes with security for subscriptions. The capital stock is entitled to representation in director elections.

classification.title: AN ACT to create a Board of Commissioners of the Sinking Fund of Bracken County
classification.summary: This act establishes a Board of Commissioners for the Sinking Fund of Bracken County. It outlines the election process for commissioners, their terms of office, the appointment of a treasurer, the treasurer's bond requirements, and the board's reporting duties. The sheriff is tasked with collecting ad valorem taxes levied by the county court for debt repayment, with powers similar to those for collecting state revenue.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial administration of Bracken County, specifically the creation of a board to manage a sinking fund and the collection of taxes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The act deals with governmental and financial matters applicable to all residents of Bracken County. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the Charter of the JeffersonRailway Company, approved January 11th, 1867.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Jefferson Railway Company, expanding the territory within which the company can exercise its corporate powers. The new limits extend from points on the western line of Louisville's tax limits, between Market and Broadway streets (if extended west), to any point on the Ohio River in Jefferson County.

Therefore, neither act is classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the Fulton County Agricultural Society, outlining its corporators, powers, capital stock, and election of directors. It grants the society the ability to contract, sue and be sued, acquire and manage land (up to twenty-five acres) and personal estate, and adopt a corporate seal. The capital stock is divided into $50 shares, and the society will be governed by eleven directors elected annually.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Seth Parker and others.
classification.summary: This act addresses a land grant error from 1865. Madison Thomas was granted 56 certificates for 200 acres each, totaling 11,200 acres of vacant land in Lewis County. This act aims to remedy this situation.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act for the benefit of Garrard County.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning Garrard County. It allows the Garrard county court to issue bonds up to $20,000 for courthouse construction, in addition to $15,000 already authorized. The interest rate on these bonds can be up to 10% annually. The court's power to levy taxes is increased to 30 cents per $100 of taxable property. Additionally, the county judge is authorized to appoint four commissioners instead of three to oversee construction.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to financial matters and public building construction for Garrard County. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act incorporates the Louisville Market House Company, granting it the power to establish and operate a market house in Louisville, Kentucky. It outlines the company's capital structure, governance by a board of directors, and the ability to lease or sell market stalls. The act also specifies provisions for borrowing money and setting rules for stall leases, including forfeiture for non-payment of rent.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the incorporation of a private company to build and manage a market house. It details the financial structure, governance, and operational aspects of the company, such as selling or leasing market stalls. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1867'.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Merchants and Traders' Insurance Company.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Merchants and Traders' Insurance Company in Louisville, Kentucky. It outlines the corporators' names, corporate powers, succession period (thirty years), and the company's ability to hold, purchase, sell, and sue. It also specifies the capital stock, how it is to be paid and secured, and prohibits the company from engaging in trading as merchants. The act also includes provisions regarding the taxation of the company's property by the city of Louisville and states that the act is in force from its passage date.

classification.summary: This act legalizes transfers of property and franchises related to the Jefferson and Brownsboro Turnpike Road Company. It re-establishes the company as a body politic and corporate for the purpose of constructing and completing a turnpike road from Louisville into Oldham county. The act also grants the company the power to increase its capital stock.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the reorganization and corporate powers of the Jefferson and Brownsboro Turnpike Road Company. It focuses on the legal and financial aspects of the company's operations, including property transfers, corporate structure, and capital stock. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Jefferson and Brownsboro Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Jefferson and Brownsboro Turnpike Road Company, granting it powers to acquire property, hold meetings, issue stock certificates, appoint officers, and construct a toll road. It specifically details the location of a toll gate on a connecting road and states that tolls collected from this gate should be kept separate from other company funds. Dividends from these tolls are to be distributed equally among stockholders of this branch road.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation and operational aspects of a turnpike road company. It outlines the company's powers, election of officers, stock issuance, and the construction and management of a road, including the erection of a toll gate. While it mentions keeping tolls separate and distributing dividends, there is no mention of race or any provisions that would suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville and Eastern Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act details amendments to the charter of the Louisville and Eastern Turnpike Road Company, including provisions for stockholder representation on the board, changes to the eastern terminus of a branch road, specifications for road grading and width, and procedures for acquiring land and materials for construction. It outlines the process for eminent domain through a writ of ad quod damnum.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to the charter of a turnpike road company. It discusses the company's operations, land acquisition, and construction specifications. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1867.

classification.title: AN ACT to charter Jeffersontown, in Jefferson county.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the inhabitants within a specific area of Jefferson County as the town of Jeffersontown, granting it corporate powers. It also outlines provisions for the president and directors of a company, including calling meetings, keeping records, making calls upon stockholders, and fixing rates of toll on roads. The act specifies that toll rates should not exceed those of the Louisville and Bardstown turnpike company and details how half tolls can be charged based on road completion and grading.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct sections. The first section (Sections 11-14) details the operational procedures and powers of a company's president and directors, including meeting quorum, record-keeping, calls on stockholders, and setting toll rates for roads. The second section (Chapter 1109) is an act to incorporate Jeffersontown in Jefferson County. Neither section contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Jeffersontown
classification.summary: This act details the governance of Jeffersontown, establishing a board of five trustees elected annually by qualified voters. It outlines their powers, including the ability to pass ordinances, levy taxes (with specific limitations and exemptions for females and males under twenty-one), and elect town officers. The act also specifies the qualifications for trustees and the process for their election and oath-taking.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the incorporation and governance of the town of Jeffersontown. It outlines the structure of its municipal government, including the election and powers of a board of trustees, as well as the town's ability to levy taxes and pass ordinances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1867.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Town of Maysville
classification.summary: This act outlines the powers and responsibilities of the board of trustees for the town of Maysville. It grants them the authority to pass ordinances to suppress various establishments like tippling houses, houses of ill-fame, gambling houses, and disorderly houses, as well as regulate the retail of liquors without a license. The board can also license shows and exhibitions and enforce these ordinances through fines. Additionally, it details the qualifications for voters, the initial board of trustees, and the powers of the town marshal.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate a town and details its municipal governance. It grants the town board of trustees the power to regulate various businesses and public order within the town limits. While it mentions suppressing 'tippling-houses, houses of ill-fame, bawdy houses, gambling houses, disorderly houses,' and regulating the sale of liquors, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The powers granted are general municipal powers and do not appear to be designed to discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It specifies the width of the road, the process for its inspection, and the rates of toll for various users and livestock. The act also details the procedures for filling vacancies in the company's leadership.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act of incorporation for a turnpike road company. It details the operational aspects of the company, including its governance, property rights, and the collection of tolls. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate a Turnpike Road from Mitchellsburg, in Boyle County, to the Town of Liberty, in Casey County
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Mitchellsburg and Liberty Turnpike Company to construct a macadamized road between Mitchellsburg and Liberty. It outlines the company's powers, capital stock, and appointment of commissioners. It also details toll rates for various vehicles and exemptions for ministers, children going to school, individuals attending church on Sundays, and funeral processions. The act specifies that provisions of the Revised Statutes regarding turnpike companies not inconsistent with this act shall be part hereof and that the act is in force from its passage.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the incorporation of a turnpike company and the regulation of tolls. It lists specific exemptions from paying tolls, such as for ministers, children going to school, and funeral processions. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would disproportionately affect a particular racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Louisville Building Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Louisville Building Association, outlining its governance, powers, and limitations. It details the election of directors, voting procedures, the role of the president, and the association's authority to purchase real estate, erect buildings, borrow money, and receive deposits. It explicitly prohibits the association from engaging in the traffic of lumber, manufacturing building materials, milling, or general merchandise, restricting its business to real estate transactions and building erection. The act also establishes procedures for stock transfers, corporate liens, and the forfeiture of the charter under specific conditions, while providing for the validity of elections held outside designated dates.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational guidelines for the Louisville Building Association. It covers aspects such as director elections, voting, financial powers, and business limitations, specifically focusing on real estate transactions and building construction. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature in 1867. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate governance and has no racial component.

his license .

shallpermitor sufferany gaming or bettingin any house

§ 4. The board of trustees of said town shall have the

court: Provided, That in allcases where the fine exceeds

prisonment ,the defendent shallbe entitledto an appeal to

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Harrodsburg
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Harrodsburg by granting the police court exclusive jurisdiction over offenses within town limits with penalties not exceeding one hundred dollars or twenty days' imprisonment. It also grants the board of trustees the right to issue coffee-house licenses, set taxes for them, and suspend licenses. The act also establishes the election of a town attorney and outlines the compensation and fee structure for court cases.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administrative and judicial functions of the town of Harrodsburg, specifically concerning the jurisdiction of its police court, the licensing of coffee-houses, and the election of a town attorney. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The penalties and procedures described apply generally to all individuals within the town's jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It specifies the company's ability to sue and be sued, contract, acquire and manage property up to $100,000, and use a corporate seal. It also defines the roles and terms of the president, vice president, directors, treasurer, and secretary, and the process for stock subscription and assignment.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Henderson Fair Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Henderson Fair Company, outlining its organizational structure, meeting procedures, powers of the board of directors, and the ability to hold annual fairs for the exhibition of stock, agricultural implements, and products, as well as horse racing. It also grants the board the power to make rules, fix entry and gate fees, appoint police, and license vendors on fairgrounds, with an exception for the sale of spirituous liquors.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation and operational procedures of the Henderson Fair Company. It outlines the powers and responsibilities of its directors, the conduct of meetings, and the organization of fairs. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted, such as licensing vendors and appointing police, are general in nature and do not appear to be racially motivated. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Richmond Manufacturing Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Richmond Manufacturing Association, outlining its governance structure, election procedures for officers, powers of the president and directors, quorum requirements, and liability. It specifies the term of office for existing officers, the process for electing new officers, and allows for the increase or decrease of directors. It also permits other counties to elect a director if their citizens subscribe to a certain number of shares. The association's affairs, estate, and contracts are managed by the president and directors, who can make by-laws and appoint officers. The association is liable for its debts.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the incorporation and operational procedures of the Richmond Manufacturing Association. It focuses on corporate governance, elections, and financial responsibilities. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The category is 'other' as it pertains to corporate law rather than specific social or civil rights categories. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V1-1867.pdf' and the year '1867'.

The second act (Chapter 1156) grants an extension to a former sheriff to collect revenue. The third act (Chapter 1157) grants an extension to an administrator of a late sheriff to collect tax and fee bills. None of these acts mention race or have any apparent discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, they are not Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Glasgow and Barren County Turnpike Company
classification.summary: This act grants the Glasgow and Barren County Turnpike Company the authority to construct and repair various turnpike and gravel roads in Barren County, Kentucky. It also outlines provisions for toll collection, penalties for avoiding tolls, and empowers the Barren County court to subscribe to the company's stock. The act details the routes the company can build and repair, including roads towards Burksville, Tompkinsville, Edmonton, Greensburg, and existing roads to Allen and Hart county lines. It also grants the company rights to acquire land, use public ways, and collect subscriptions. The county court is authorized to levy taxes for stock subscription, subject to voter approval.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the construction and funding of turnpike roads in Barren County, Kentucky. It details the powers and responsibilities of a turnpike company and the local county court. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on infrastructure development and financial mechanisms. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Company," approved March 10, 1856, and to authorize the county court of Barren county to subscribe stock in the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Company.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Barren County Court to subscribe $100,000 to the capital stock of the Louisville and Bardstown Turnpike Company. It outlines the process for determining voter approval, including the appointment of commissioners to count qualified voters from the 1865 assessor's books and verify if a majority have petitioned for the stock subscription. If approved, the county judge is to issue bonds to the company, with a payment schedule for the principal and annual interest.

However, the primary purpose of the act is town incorporation and governance, not direct racial segregation or discrimination in the same vein as other Jim Crow laws. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Allensville
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of Allensville broad powers to govern the town, including making conveyances, opening and improving streets, levying taxes (ad valorem and poll), taxing specific businesses and activities like auction sales, shows, and exhibitions, and licensing various establishments such as taverns, groceries, retailers of spirituous liquors, and places of amusement. It also outlines penalties for violating by-laws and ordinances, including specific penalties for riots, unlawful assemblies, breaches of peace, disturbance of religious worship, tippling-houses, and gambling-houses. A specific provision requires the treasurer to pay $10 per tavern license to the jury fund for Todd County and repeals any prior authority of the county court to license taverns, merchants, and druggists for selling spirituous liquors.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the powers and responsibilities of the town trustees of Allensville, including their authority to levy taxes, license businesses, and set penalties for violations of town ordinances. While it mentions licensing taverns and retailers of spirituous liquors, and sets a fee for tavern licenses to be paid to the jury fund, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The penalties for various offenses do not appear to be racially differentiated. Therefore, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Establish a Police Court in the Town of Allensville
classification.summary: This act establishes a police court in the town of Allensville, Kentucky, and appoints S. T. Fortune as judge and W. M. Bagby as marshal. It outlines their terms of office, the oaths they must take, and the bond the marshal must provide. The act also specifies that the establishment of this police court does not exempt individuals from obtaining necessary licenses from the Todd county court or from paying required state taxes and fees.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute focuses on the administrative and judicial structure of the town's police court. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend the charter of the town of Allensville, in Todd county.
classification.summary: This act details the election and duties of a judge for the Allensville police court. It outlines the judge's term of office, how vacancies are filled, and their powers as a conservator of the peace throughout Todd County. The judge's jurisdiction is defined as being the same as a justice of the peace, with specific provisions for criminal cases and offenses under town ordinances. The act also grants the judge powers to issue various legal writs and take depositions, with certain limitations on defendants being compelled to attend trials outside their justice's district. It also covers jurisdiction over suits against town officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the establishment and powers of a local police court judge in Allensville, Todd County. It outlines the judge's election, term, jurisdiction, and specific judicial powers, such as issuing writs and handling cases related to town ordinances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a local administrative and judicial matter, not related to Jim Crow segregation or disenfranchisement.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Kentucky Coal and Iron Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Kentucky Coal and Iron Company, outlining its capital stock, management structure, and operational powers. It specifies that the company can receive land and mineral rights for stock subscriptions, manage its affairs through a board of directors, and issue stock. The act explicitly states that no banking privileges will be granted. It also allows the company to keep offices within or without the state, provided the principal office is in Kentucky, and to conduct business anywhere in the United States not conflicting with state or federal laws. The act was approved on February 1, 1867.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Fleming
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Fleming powers related to public works and property maintenance. These powers include clearing streets, maintaining water sources, abating nuisances, regulating storage of materials, ensuring property owners maintain sidewalks and streets in front of their lots, and the ability to have such work done at the owner's expense if they fail to comply, with a lien on the property for costs. It also allows for street paving upon voter request.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the powers of a town's board of trustees concerning public works, property maintenance, and street improvements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The powers granted are general municipal functions applicable to all residents. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Augusta, in the County of Bracken
classification.summary: This act grants the trustees of the town of Augusta the power to tax hotel and saloon keepers who sell spirituous or vinous liquors in quantities less than a quart. They can also suspend or suppress such licenses and close bars on Sundays and holidays. Additionally, druggists, merchants, and others selling liquors in quantities greater than a quart can be taxed. The act also outlines the process for appointing an assessor to list taxable property and individuals within the town, with penalties for non-compliance. Finally, it grants the trustees the power to levy an ad valorem tax.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales, taxation, and local governance within the town of Augusta. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The powers granted to the trustees are general in nature and apply to all individuals and businesses within the town's jurisdiction, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Town of Williamsburg, in Whitley County.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Town of Williamsburg in Whitley County, defining its corporate limits and establishing a board of five trustees to govern the town. The trustees are to be elected by qualified voters, with any three forming a quorum. They are empowered to contract, sue, and be sued, and must keep a journal of their proceedings. The act also specifies that individuals licensed to vend spirituous or vinous liquors are ineligible to serve as trustees during the continuation of their license. Finally, it repeals any conflicting prior acts.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act to incorporate the Town of Williamsburg. It outlines the governance structure, including the election and powers of trustees, and sets a restriction on liquor vendors serving as trustees. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the content is clearly about municipal incorporation and has no racial component.

It grants county judges or justices of the peace in Clinton County the power to issue arrest warrants for violators of town by-laws. Sheriffs, coroners, jailers, or constables can make arrests anywhere in the county. Violators unable to pay fines will be committed to jail until discharged. The by-laws of the town will extend half a mile beyond its corporate limits for breaches of good order and regulations.

Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence, and the category is null.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the town of Ghent."
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Ghent. It allows for special elections if regular elections are missed, specifies voting eligibility for free white male citizens over 21 residing in town for 60 days, and property owners regardless of residency. It also outlines the election of the police judge, marshal, and assessor, and grants the trustees power to sell public property with the approval of property holders.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'free white male citizens' as eligible voters. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Crab Orchard Cemetery Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Crab Orchard Cemetery Company, granting it the power to purchase, hold, and sell land (not exceeding twenty acres) for burial purposes within one and a half miles of Crab Orchard. The land is to be free from interference by public improvements and taxes. The company's affairs are to be managed by a board of six directors, elected annually by shareholders. The board has the power to erect buildings for the corporation's use.

classification.summary: This act details the division of the city of Henderson into four wards, the distribution of governmental powers into legislative, executive, and judicial departments, and the eligibility requirements for common councilmen. Eligibility requires being a free white male citizen of the United States, at least twenty-five years old, a resident of Henderson County for two years and the city for six months, and a bona fide resident of the ward for which they are chosen. It also prohibits holding other public offices.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that a common councilman must be a 'free white male citizen of the United States.' This is a clear racial qualification for holding public office, which is a hallmark of discriminatory practices during the Jim Crow era. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. The category is 'other' as it pertains to governance and eligibility for office rather than specific areas like education or public accommodations. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1867'.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Henderson
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Henderson, Kentucky. It outlines eligibility requirements for members of the common council, including restrictions on holding contracts with the city or offices in corporations that have contracts with the city. It also details rules for representation, ward creation, election terms, and the publication of council proceedings. Stockholders in corporations are eligible but cannot vote on matters affecting their corporation. Officers in the militia are explicitly eligible.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the governance and administration of the city of Henderson, specifically concerning the composition and election of its common council. It details eligibility criteria, representation, and procedural matters. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language used is neutral and administrative. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Ordinances and Council Proceedings
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1867 outlines the procedures for publishing ordinances, the digestion and printing of general ordinances, meeting schedules for the common council, compensation and privileges of council members, quorum requirements, enforcement of order, and voting procedures for ordinances. It specifies that ordinances for street improvements, taxes, payment of money, or of a penal nature require a two-thirds vote and recorded yeas and nays, while other ordinances require a majority vote of members present.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of city governance, specifically concerning the creation, publication, and voting on ordinances by a common council. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1867), indicating it's an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This act grants the City of Louisville various powers, including the authority to levy taxes, borrow money, pay debts and expenses, establish quarantine and health regulations, manage nuisances, provide water and lighting, improve streets and public grounds, build bridges and sewers, and establish and regulate police, markets, and market inspectors. It also includes provisions for condemning land for public improvements.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text outlines the general powers and responsibilities of the City of Louisville's government. It covers a broad range of municipal functions such as taxation, public health, infrastructure development, and law enforcement. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Louisville
classification.summary: This act grants the city of Louisville broad powers to regulate various aspects of commerce and public life within the city. These include establishing public scales and standard weights and measures, inspecting and measuring lumber, hay, coal, and provisions like pork, bacon, flour, meal, and whisky. It also allows for the inspection of butter and lard, regulation of meat vending, prevention of forestalling, appointment of city officers, fixing compensation and fees, establishing workhouses and houses of correction, licensing ferries, levying a poll tax, and preventing riots. The provisions regarding inspection of goods for shipment outside the state are noted as requiring owner or agent consent.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines general municipal powers related to commerce, public safety, and taxation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is on economic regulation and city administration. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

It also outlines the executive department, vesting supreme executive power in a mayor elected for a two-year term. The mayor's responsibilities include heading the police, enforcing laws, presiding over council meetings, and requiring information from city officers. The act also specifies the mayor's compensation and eligibility requirements.

It defines the role of the mayor, the common council, and establishes provisions for defining and punishing vagrancy. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Henderson
classification.summary: This act details the powers and duties of the Mayor and the establishment and jurisdiction of the Henderson City Court. It outlines the eligibility requirements for the City Judge, including being a free white male citizen, at least twenty-five years of age, a licensed practicing lawyer for four years, and a resident of the city for at least one year. The court has exclusive original jurisdiction over ordinance violations and exclusive jurisdiction over pleas of the Commonwealth within the city limits, except for felonies.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that to be eligible for the office of judge of the city court, a person must be a 'free white male citizen'. This is a direct racial and gender qualification, characteristic of discriminatory laws of the era. While it doesn't directly enforce segregation or disenfranchisement, it establishes a racial prerequisite for holding a judicial office, which is a form of racial exclusion. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the racial requirement is explicit. The category is null because the statute primarily concerns the structure and qualifications for a city court and its judge, rather than a specific area like education, housing, or public accommodations. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1867'. The racial indicator is 'explicit' due to the direct mention of 'free white male citizen'.

classification.summary: This act details various provisions for the city of Henderson, including the removal of city assessors for malfeasance, the oath of office for city officers, the establishment of voting places, the appointment of election officers, the voting method (vivavoce, with exceptions for the deaf), election hours, and qualifications for voters. Specifically, it states that qualified voters must be free white male citizens over twenty-one years of age, resident in the city and state for specified periods, and bona fide residents of their ward.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly defines qualified voters as 'Every freewhite male citizen'. This is a clear racial and gender-based restriction on voting rights. While it is not a Jim Crow law in the sense of post-Reconstruction segregation laws, it is a discriminatory voting law that falls under the broader umbrella of disenfranchisement practices of the era. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence due to the explicit racial qualifier. The category is 'voting' due to the focus on voter qualifications and election procedures. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year '1867'.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the Christian County Court to issue Bonds for the purpose of building a Court-house, and to establish a Sinking Fund to liquidate the said Bonds.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Christian County Court to issue bonds up to $50,000 to build a courthouse. It also establishes a sinking fund, managed by appointed commissioners, to repay these bonds. The fund will be financed through an annual ad valorem tax and poll tax, with specific amounts to be set aside annually until the bonds are liquidated. The commissioners are tasked with loaning out the sinking fund money and are held accountable for its management.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financing and construction of a courthouse in Christian County, Kentucky. It details the authorization for issuing bonds, the establishment and management of a sinking fund to repay these bonds, and the appointment of commissioners to oversee the fund. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act for the relief of the Sheriff of Franklin County
classification.summary: This act provides relief to the Sheriff of Franklin County and his sureties by crediting a judgment against them. The judgment was related to the sheriff's failure to pay revenue for 1865 and outstanding taxes from 1862-1865. The act allows for credits on the judgment for damages and interest, and directs the county judge to inspect tax books and allow credits for uncollected, improperly assessed, or delinquent taxes, with the Auditor of Public Accounts to be notified of these credits. The county judge is also to re-list collectable outstanding taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses a financial matter concerning a sheriff and tax collection in Franklin County. It details a judgment against the sheriff for unpaid revenue and outstanding taxes, and outlines a process for providing relief and adjusting the debt based on equity. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the content is clearly financial and administrative, with no racial component.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Vanceburg, Salt Lick, Tollsboro, and Maysville Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Vanceburg, Salt Lick, Tollsboro, and Maysville Turnpike Road Company. It outlines the process for subscribing to stock, electing a president and directors, and grants the company powers to lay out, survey, and construct the road. It also specifies regulations for the road's elevation, grade, width, stone coverage, and the placement of toll gates. The act further details the rates of toll that can be charged, which are to be consistent with those allowed to the Maysville, Washington, Paris, and Lexington turnpike road company. Finally, it describes the procedures for calling on subscribers for payment of their stock and the delivery of stock certificates.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and operational procedures for a turnpike road company. It covers aspects such as stock subscriptions, election of officers, road construction, and toll collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Maysville and Big Sandy Turnpike Road Company," approved March 10, 1860, and to authorize the construction of a turnpike road in Lewis and Mason counties.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the construction of a turnpike road in Lewis and Mason counties, Kentucky. It outlines the process for land acquisition, material sourcing, and the establishment of toll gates. The company is empowered to occupy public roads and private lands for construction purposes, with provisions for compensation to landowners. Tolls can be collected once a certain length of the road is completed. The act also details the appointment of gatekeepers, a clerk, and a treasurer.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the process for building a turnpike road, including land acquisition and toll collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the legislature in 1867.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the 10th article of the Charter of the City of Louisville.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Louisville's public school system. It allows non-resident taxpayers to send their children to city schools free of tuition, but explicitly prohibits children of the African race from attending schools with white children. It also mandates that school taxes paid by persons of the African race be kept as a separate fund and used for the education of African race children within the city, potentially establishing separate schools for them.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates the segregation of students by race in the public schools of Louisville. It states that 'nothing in this act shall permit or authorize the general council of the city of Louisville, or board of trustees of said public schools, to suffer children of the African race to become pupils of said school with white children.' Furthermore, it requires that school taxes paid by persons of the African race be kept in a separate fund and used for the education of African race children, potentially establishing separate schools. This clearly falls under Jim Crow legislation related to education. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'explicit' because it directly names the 'African race' and 'white children'.

1867 . CHAPTER 1389 .
AN ACT to extend the limitsof the Town of Benton, MarshallCounty .
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the corporate limitsof the town of Benton ,in
the county of Marshall,be ,and they are hereby ,extended
so as to include the ground within the following boundary ,
viz: beginning at the town line on Lick creek, and run-
ning thence up said creek, with its meanderings ,to the
Wadesboro road, and thence north with said road to its
intersection with Main street .
§2 . That the countycourt of Marshall countybe,and
itishereby , authorized and required to divide and lay off
said ground intolots,with streetsand alleys, such as in its
discretionmay be deemed most suitableand convenient .
§ 3. That the trusteesof saidtown are hereby required
to have made out and recorded,by order of said court,a
plat of said town ,including the addition,giving the num-
bers and sizeof alllots,and the names ,width , and length
of allstreetsand alleys; and in surveying the ground and
making out the plat,due respectshallbe given to the
numbers and size of the lots, and names ,width ,and local-
itiesof the streetsand alleys,as establishedby the original
plat, which was destroyed in and with the burning of the
clerks' officesof said county . The trusteesare authorizedto take and hear proof in regard to the same . The said
plat,when recorded, shallbe evidenceof the numbers,
size,and localityof the lots, and of the names ,width ,and
localitiesof the streetsand alleys.
§ 4. That thecorporateauthoritiesof saidtown be,and
they are hereby ,empowered and required to provide for
thepoor of saidtown ,and to keep the streetsand alleys
in order .
§5 . Before said additionto the town is made ,ten days'
previous noticein writing shall be posted by the trustees
at the court-house door ; and in other respects article5 ,
chapter 100,of the Revised Statutes,shallgovern .
§ 6. All expensesincidentto the addition,the survey,
plat,and recording,shallbe paidby the trustees.
§7. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved February 14, 1867 .

The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Lexington
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the City of Lexington, establishing it as a corporate body, defining the powers of the mayor and city council, and outlining the process for ward division and city elections. It specifies that only 'free white male inhabitants over twenty-one years of age' are eligible to vote in city elections.

This is a clear indicator of racial discrimination in voting rights, a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow. classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the City of Lexington
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for municipal elections in the city of Lexington, Kentucky. It details the qualifications for voters, the process for appointing election inspectors, the methods for conducting elections, and the procedures for filling vacancies in elected city offices. The statute specifies that qualified voters must be citizens of Kentucky, inhabitants of the city, and have resided there for six calendar months prior to the election. It also describes how votes for mayor, councilmen, city attorney, treasurer, and captain of the night-watch are to be cast, tallied, and certified.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the process for municipal elections in Lexington, Kentucky. It details voter qualifications, election procedures, and how to fill vacancies. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on the administrative aspects of local elections. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also details the process for grading streets for water drainage or preparation for paving.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and related to urban infrastructure development. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An act to incorporate the city of Lexington," approved March 11, 1867.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Lexington. The bonds are to be secured by mortgages on the railroad property.

classification.title: Poor and Work-houses
classification.summary: This section grants the city the power and duty to establish and manage poorhouses and workhouses. It specifies that the indigent poor should be provided for comfortably at the corporation's expense, managed by appointed individuals in distinct apartments from the infirm and indigent. It also details the confinement of common beggars, vagrants, and those sentenced for short periods by the Lexington city court for minor offenses or breaches of peace. These individuals are to be employed in labor and perform tasks as directed by city ordinances, with commitment to the work-house requiring a warrant from the Lexington city court.
classification.reasoning: The statute outlines the establishment and management of poorhouses and workhouses by the city of Lexington. It details the provisions for the indigent poor and the confinement and labor of beggars, vagrants, and those convicted of minor offenses. While it mentions distinct apartments for the infirm and indigent, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The focus is on social welfare and penal labor for those deemed undesirable or convicted of minor offenses, without any racial segregation or discrimination indicated in the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This section of the act grants the mayor the power to investigate and arrest individuals involved in unlawful assemblages, specifically mentioning gatherings for gaming, drinking, or any purpose not allowed by law. classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'assemblages of negroesor others, for the purpose of gaming, drinking, or for any purpose not allowed by law' and 'where the said assemblage shall, or may be constituted partly of white and partly of black population'. This direct reference to racial composition in the context of regulating gatherings, particularly those deemed 'unlawful', is a strong indicator of Jim Crow-era legislation aimed at controlling and potentially suppressing Black populations and their interactions with white populations. The power granted to the mayor to arrest individuals in such assemblages, especially when mixed-race, aligns with the discriminatory practices of the Jim Crow era. It mandates that individuals convicted of vagrancy be sentenced to labor in the city workhouse for a period of three to six months, determined by a jury. Justices of the peace are required to recognize individuals charged with vagrancy to appear before the city court instead of the circuit court.

While race is not explicitly mentioned, vagrancy laws during the Jim Crow era were often disproportionately applied to Black individuals, serving as a mechanism for forced labor and social control.
